title: Documentation and AI assistance

publication.changeSummary: Topic-specific draft for Documentation and AI assistance; requires human source, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: ACM Code of Ethics and Professional Conduct; 2018; section applicable to Documentation and AI assistance
sources.claim: Documentation and AI assistance should answer a bounded question, expose assumptions, cite applicable versions, and be verified against the problem, authoritative sources, and executable tests.

sources.locator: CS2023 Software Development Fundamentals; August 2023; section applicable to Documentation and AI assistance
sources.claim: Documentation and AI assistance should answer a bounded question, expose assumptions, cite applicable versions, and be verified against the problem, authoritative sources, and executable tests.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Documentation and AI assistance should answer a bounded question, expose assumptions, cite applicable versions, and be verified against the problem, authoritative sources, and executable tests.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A learner asks for the documented return behavior of one library call, checks official versioned docs, then writes boundary tests before using the suggestion. Your goal is to evaluate an assisted answer against a source, tests and the stated problem. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Debugging and testing loop; Using feedback. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Operational model
sections.body: Documentation and AI assistance should answer a bounded question, expose assumptions, cite applicable versions, and be verified against the problem, authoritative sources, and executable tests. The learner should use this model to predict an observable result rather than merely repeat terminology.

sections.heading: Boundary and failure point
sections.body: Confident wording is not evidence, generated code can leak secrets or violate constraints, and a correct snippet in one version or context may be wrong in another. A correct explanation therefore includes the applicable assumptions and avoids this common mistake: A fluent answer from documentation search or an AI tool can be submitted without verification if it looks conventional.

includes: Ask bounded questions, verify claims and retain ownership of reasoning and submitted work.
Evaluate an assisted answer against a source, tests and the stated problem.
excludes: Confident wording is not evidence, generated code can leak secrets or violate constraints, and a correct snippet in one version or context may be wrong in another.

outcomes: Evaluate an assisted answer against a source, tests and the stated problem.

examples.title: Documentation and AI assistance: direct case
examples.situation: A learner asks for the documented return behavior of one library call, checks official versioned docs, then writes boundary tests before using the suggestion.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the starting evidence: A learner asks for the documented return behavior of one library call, checks official versioned docs, then writes boundary tests before using the suggestion.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.

Step 3 — Check the result against this correction: Retain ownership by narrowing the request, checking source and version, testing normal and boundary behavior, and explaining every submitted decision.
examples.result: The case supports the declared outcome because Retain ownership by narrowing the request, checking source and version, testing normal and boundary behavior, and explaining every submitted decision.

examples.title: Documentation and AI assistance: boundary case
examples.situation: An AI-proposed fix is compared with the assignment constraints and rejected because it imports a forbidden library despite passing one example.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed context: An AI-proposed fix is compared with the assignment constraints and rejected because it imports a forbidden library despite passing one example.

Step 2 — Keep this boundary explicit: Confident wording is not evidence, generated code can leak secrets or violate constraints, and a correct snippet in one version or context may be wrong in another.
Step 3 — Reject the documented misconception: A fluent answer from documentation search or an AI tool can be submitted without verification if it looks conventional.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Evaluate an assisted answer against a source, tests and the stated problem.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: A fluent answer from documentation search or an AI tool can be submitted without verification if it looks conventional.
misconceptions.correction: Retain ownership by narrowing the request, checking source and version, testing normal and boundary behavior, and explaining every submitted decision.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using the direct and boundary scenarios, explain why this belief fails: A fluent answer from documentation search or an AI tool can be submitted without verification if it looks conventional.

analogy.example: Sports coaching can model Documentation and AI assistance: study a play, attempt it, use specific feedback, retry independently, and credit the people or material that helped.
usefulFor: Connecting explanation, feedback, independent transfer, assistance, and integrity
limitations: Learning evidence and reuse permissions are governed by explicit course and software rules, not only informal coaching norms.

scaffold: Line 2: state the part of the core rule that applies.
Line 3: give the result and reject the common mistake.

recap.summary: You should now be able to evaluate an assisted answer against a source, tests and the stated problem. Keep this boundary in view: Confident wording is not evidence, generated code can leak secrets or violate constraints, and a correct snippet in one version or context may be wrong in another.
retrievalPrompts: Explain Documentation and AI assistance without using the optional analogy and include one observable consequence.
Why is this statement incorrect: A fluent answer from documentation search or an AI tool can be submitted without verification if it looks conventional.
Apply the model to this case from memory: An AI-proposed fix is compared with the assignment constraints and rejected because it imports a forbidden library despite passing one example.
recap.nextReviewPrompt: Next: continue to “Integrity and attribution”. Before moving on, answer one retrieval prompt without reopening the explanation.
